Fast exponentiation Horner’s scheme is optimal for polynomials: … but what about just a single large power? Big exponents used in public-key cryptography. Fast exponentiation Fast exponentiation Θ(N) is too slow: ●N ≈ 10600 Fast exponentiation Θ(N) is too slow: ●N ≈ 10600, log10 N = 600. Θ(log N) is the number of digits (space) needed to write down N. Several algorithms based on repeated squaring: Fast exponentiation An iterative approach might look like: ● Decompose N into binary ● Multiply the “1” powers Fast exponentiation Fast exponentiation How would we do this recursively? Θ(n2) operations Karatsuba’s algorithm It is possible to do o(n2) operations using divide-and-conquer Each part A, B, C, D has n/2 digits It is possible to do o(n2) operations using divide-and-conquer Each part A, B, C, D has n/2 digits Karatsuba’s algorithm Karatsuba’s algorithm It is possible to do o(n2) operations using divide-and-conquer Each part A, B, C, D has n/2 digits A ﬁrst attempt… A ﬁrst attempt… A ﬁrst attempt… Some helper functions A ﬁrst attempt… Runtime would be: T(n) = 4 T(n/2) + Θ(n) A ﬁrst attempt… Runtime would be: T(n) = 4 T(n/2) + Θ(n) = Θ(n2) by master theorem. A ﬁrst attempt… Runtime would be: T(n) = 4 T(n/2) + Θ(n) = Θ(n2) by master theorem. No improvement??? Karatsuba’s algorithm Karatsuba’s brilliant idea: BC + AD = AC + BD - (A - B)(C - D) Karatsuba’s algorithm How many recursive calls now? How many recursive calls now? Looks like 5, but only 3 (2 repeated) Karatsuba’s algorithm Karatsuba’s algorithm Runtime would be: T(n) = 3T(n/2) + Θ(n) Karatsuba’s algorithm Runtime would be: T(n) = 3T(n/2) + Θ(n) = by master theorem.
